critic_raw:
Issue 1
Claim: Cross-validation performance estimates are reliable for model selection.
Why wrong: The team 'assembled a single modeling table and then applied median imputation and target encoding to the full table before creating cross-validation folds.' This ordering is the classic preprocessing leakage error. For target encoding specifically: the encoding for repair shop ID in a training fold encodes mean claim outcome across all two years, including future folds' outcomes. The cross-validation accuracy estimate is optimistic because each fold's 'test' portion is partially visible through the encoding.
Evidence: Fit target encoding within each training fold separately. Compare leakage-free cross-validation AUC to the reported AUC.

Issue 2
Claim: The 80% recall threshold is independently validated because it is an operational requirement.
Why wrong: The threshold is an operational target, but it is identified on the held-out test set. There are typically multiple threshold values that achieve exactly 80% recall on any finite dataset. The one chosen is the lowest threshold (highest precision) that achieves 80% recall in-sample — this is threshold optimization on test data. Production recall and precision at this threshold will regress toward the true relationship, not the test-set-optimized estimate.
Evidence: Apply the threshold selected on the test set to a fresh time-forward hold-out or use k-fold validation of the threshold across the training set to estimate the expected precision at 80% recall.

Issue 3
Claim: The AutoML results are comparable to the logistic regression baseline.
Why wrong: The logistic regression was tuned with 'nested cross-validation' under its original project protocol and retains its original validation-set threshold. The AutoML system was searched with a leaky CV protocol and has a test-set-selected threshold. These are not equivalent protocols. The comparison systematically advantages the AutoML system through two separate protocol differences.
Evidence: Rerun logistic regression under the same fold-specific-target-encoding protocol and select its threshold on the same validation split. Compare on the same independent test set.
defender_raw: Target encoding is a widely used feature engineering technique that is known to improve model performance on high-cardinality categorical data. The cross-validation was designed to select the best pipeline, and the held-out test set was reserved for final evaluation. The 80% recall threshold was not chosen to maximize precision — it was set operationally. The AutoML search covered both gradient boosting and linear pipelines, giving the logistic approach a fair chance within the search space.
adjudication_raw: The defender conflates target encoding as a valid technique (true) with applying it correctly in cross-validation (false here). On Issue 2: the defender says 80% recall was 'set operationally' but the threshold that achieves exactly 80% on the test set is still selected on the test set. These are different concepts. On Issue 3: the defender says the AutoML search 'covered linear pipelines' — but this doesn't address the baseline's asymmetric threshold-selection protocol. All three issues stand. Verdict: critique_wins.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: target encoding fitted on full table before fold creation — cross-validation AUC is optimistic
Issue 2: threshold selected on test set even though recall target is operationally fixed
Issue 3: logistic baseline under different preprocessing and threshold protocol than AutoML

empirical_test.condition: Fold-specific target encoding for AutoML; threshold selection on validation set for both systems; final evaluation on independent test set under matched protocols
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Leakage-free AutoML lift at 80% recall falls below 1 point or ROC-AUC advantage shrinks below 0.01
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Under leakage-free protocol, AutoML still shows at least 0.02 ROC-AUC improvement and 3 percentage point precision gain at 80% recall
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: ROC-AUC gain preserved but queue-level threshold gain does not replicate
